question_text: A gas is trapped in a sealed syringe. When the plunger is pushed in, the volume of the gas decreases. What happens to the pressure of the gas?

options.label: A
options.text: The pressure increases because the gas particles are forced closer together and collide with the walls more frequently

options.label: B
options.text: The pressure decreases because there is less space for the particles to move

options.label: C
options.text: The pressure stays the same because the amount of gas has not changed

options.label: D
options.text: The pressure increases because the particles move faster when compressed

explanation: When you reduce the volume of a gas in a sealed container, the gas particles are squashed into a smaller space. They collide with the walls more often per second, which causes the pressure to increase. This is why the syringe becomes harder to push.
